No Poverty.reason: The course explicitly covers "Socio-Economic Rights" (Week 12) and references the CESCR, which addresses issues like social protection and access to basic services relevant to poverty (Targets 1.3, 1.4).
evidence: CESCR, Mohamed Ben Djazia and Naouel Bellili v. Spain

Zero Hunger.reason: No explicit or inferable content in the syllabus directly addresses hunger, food security, or sustainable agriculture.

Good Health and Well-being.reason: No explicit or inferable content in the syllabus directly addresses health services, disease prevention, or well-being.

Quality Education.reason: The course, "International Laws of Human Right," inherently contributes to Target 4.7 by fostering knowledge and skills related to human rights, a core aspect of sustainable development and global citizenship.
evidence: 公民權利與基本權利之素養

Gender Equality.reason: International human rights law inherently addresses discrimination, including gender-based discrimination, aligning with Target 5.1 to end all forms of discrimination against women and girls.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: No explicit or inferable content in the syllabus directly addresses clean water, sanitation, or related environmental management.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: No explicit or inferable content in the syllabus directly addresses energy access, renewable energy, or energy efficiency.

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course explicitly covers "Modern Slavery, Business & Human Rights" (Week 10), directly addressing Target 8.7 to eradicate forced labor and modern slavery.

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: No explicit or inferable content in the syllabus directly addresses industry, innovation, or infrastructure development.

Reduced Inequalities.reason: The course explicitly addresses "Racial Discrimination & Hate Speech" (Week 11) and includes case law on indigenous communities and non-refoulement, directly supporting Targets 10.2 and 10.3.
evidence: Indigenous Communities Members of the Lhaka Honhat Association v. Argentina

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: The course's discussion of "Socio-Economic Rights" (Week 12) and the CESCR case could indirectly touch upon housing rights, which relates to Target 11.1, though the connection is minor.
evidence: Mohamed Ben Djazia and Naouel Bellili v. Spain

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: No explicit or inferable content in the syllabus directly addresses responsible consumption, production patterns, or waste management.

Climate Action.reason: The course explicitly dedicates a week to "Climate, Environment & Human Rights" (Week 13) and includes relevant case law, directly addressing climate change measures and awareness within legal frameworks (Targets 13.2, 13.3).
evidence: Verein KlimaSeniorinnen Schweiz and Others v. Switzerland

Life Below Water.reason: No explicit or inferable content in the syllabus directly addresses marine ecosystems, ocean health, or sustainable use of marine resources.

Life on Land.reason: The course's topic "Climate, Environment & Human Rights" (Week 13) and the case on indigenous land rights (Lhaka Honhat) provide an explicit, though indirect, link to the conservation and sustainable use of terrestrial ecosystems (Target 15.1).
evidence: Indigenous Communities Members of the Lhaka Honhat Association v. Argentina

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: The course is fundamentally about "International Laws of Human Right," explicitly aiming to prepare students for challenges to "peace, democracy, and the rule of law," and extensively covers human rights monitoring systems, directly aligning with Target 16.3.
evidence: peace, democracy, and the rule of law

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The course extensively covers international human rights monitoring systems and emphasizes "bridging international and national dialogues," demonstrating a strong focus on international cooperation and multi-stakeholder partnerships (Targets 17.14, 17.16).
evidence: Human Rights Monitoring System of the United Nations
co-organised by the Department of Political Science, the College of Law, and the International College of Innovation
